concepts.title: Linked List Representation
concepts.description: A linked list is represented as a sequence of nodes, where each node contains a data element and a reference (or link) to the next node in the sequence.

concepts.title: Types of Linked Lists
concepts.description: There are two main types of linked lists: Singly Linked List and Doubly Linked List.

concepts.title: Insertion and Deletion in Linked List
concepts.description: Insertion and deletion in a linked list can be done at the beginning or end of the list by updating the head or tail pointers and the corresponding links between nodes.

concepts.title: Linked List Operations
concepts.description: To remember the operations in a linked list: INSERTION - I (Insert), N (Node), S (Sequence), E (Element), R (Reference), T (Tail), I (Insertion); DELETION - D (Delete), E (Element), L (Link), E (End), T (Tail), I (Insertion), O (Overlap); TRAVERSAL - T (Traverse), R (Reference), A (Along), V (Value), E (Element), R (Reference), S (Sequence), A (Along).

concepts.title: Confusing Linked List with Array
concepts.description: Students often confuse linked lists with arrays, but remember that in a linked list, each node contains a reference to the next node, whereas in an array, elements are stored in contiguous memory locations.

concepts.title: Linked List Operations Time Complexity
concepts.description: The time complexity of linked list operations such as insertion, deletion, and traversal is O(n) in the worst case, where n is the number of nodes in the list.

concepts.title: Linked List Applications
concepts.description: Linked lists are used in applications such as memory management, database indexing, and file systems.

concepts.title: Linked List Merits and Demerits
concepts.description: Linked lists have the merit of efficient insertion and deletion, but they have the demerit of slow traversal and search times compared to arrays.
